title: Controlling Personnel Exposure: Time, Distance, Shielding, ALARA
narrationScript: Let's talk about the part of radiography that decides whether you get to do this work for thirty years. A typical field source is sixty to one hundred curies of iridium one ninety-two. Unshielded, one hundred curies puts out roughly fifty roentgen per hour at one meter. The typical occupational limit is five rem in a year. Do that arithmetic and it is sobering. Standing one meter from that unshielded source for about six minutes spends your entire annual limit. Industrial radiography is one of the very few jobs where a worker routinely directs that much radioactive material by hand. The skill that makes it a career instead of a casualty is exposure control. Everything in exposure control hangs on one simple relationship. Dose equals dose rate multiplied by time. You cannot feel radiation, but you can always attack one side of that equation or the other, and we attack it with three levers. Time. Distance. Shielding. Time is the bluntest lever. Half the time in the field means half the dose. In practice, time control is planning, not hurry. Walk the shot in a dry run while the source is locked up, so nothing gets figured out while the meter is climbing. Stage your film, your markers, and your guide tubes before the first exposure. Move with purpose near the device, and do your waiting far away. A technician who spends ten minutes of every shot inside a six millirem per hour zone picks up one millirem per shot. Multiply that by a few thousand shots a year and you see why the habits matter. Rushing is not time control. A fumbled connector or a botched shot that has to be repeated doubles your time near the source. Smooth, rehearsed work is the fastest work there is. Distance is the most powerful lever, because radiation from a compact source spreads out like spray from a nozzle. The same energy passes through a bigger and bigger area, so the intensity falls with the square of the distance. Double your distance and the dose rate drops to one quarter. Triple it and you are down to one ninth. Run it the other way and it bites. Cut your distance in half and the rate quadruples. Here is the working form. The rate at the first distance times that distance squared equals the rate at the second distance times that distance squared. Suppose your meter reads eight millirem per hour at twenty feet, and your boundary belongs at two millirem per hour. Two is one quarter of eight, so you need twice the distance. Post at forty feet and confirm with the meter. That last step is not optional. The inverse square law assumes a point source and no scatter. Real jobs have scatter off floors, walls, and the part itself, so the boundary is set where the meter says two, not where the calculator does. Shielding is the third lever. Every material absorbs gamma rays exponentially, and the convenient way to think about it is the half value layer, the thickness that cuts the dose rate in half. For iridium one ninety-two, one half value layer is about half an inch of steel, or about five millimeters of lead. A tenth value layer cuts the rate to one tenth, and it takes about three and a third half value layers to make one tenth value layer. Stack them and the numbers fall fast. Three half value layers cut one hundred sixty millirem per hour down to twenty. Two tenth value layers cut any rate by a factor of one hundred. Cobalt sixty is a harder gamma, so it needs roughly two and a half times the lead per half value layer. And remember the shielding you did not bring. A concrete wall, a steel vessel, the corner of the building. Put structure between yourself and the source whenever the cable run allows it. The collimator deserves special respect. It is shielding you carry to the shot, and in the directions it covers it can cut dose rates by a factor of ten or more, which shrinks your boundary and your own dose at the crank. That brings us to ALARA. As low as reasonably achievable. The limit of five rem per year is a ceiling, never an allowance. ALARA is the professional habit of questioning every millirem. It is choosing the collimated shot over the open one. It is cranking from the far end of the cable with a wall between you and the work. It is scheduling shots for when the shop is empty, briefing the crew before the first exposure, reading your dosimeter every day, and asking why whenever a number looks unusual. Regulators expect a documented ALARA program with management commitment and dose reviews, but the program is really a mindset. The best radiographers finish their careers with dose records that look boring. That is the goal. Boring numbers, every year, on purpose.
imagePrompt: A radiographer in coveralls and a hard hat cranks the drive cable of a gamma projector at full cable length behind a concrete block wall while a coworker holding a survey meter checks a rope-and-sign boundary across an industrial yard at dusk.

sections.heading: Exposure Control Is the Core Skill
sections.body:
A working radiography source is not a trace of radioactivity - it is typically **60 to 100 curies of Ir-192**, and the rule of thumb says Ir-192 delivers about **0.5 R/h per curie at 1 meter**. Unshielded, a 100 Ci source therefore produces roughly **50 R/h at 1 meter**. Set that against the typical occupational whole-body limit of **5 rem per year** (10 CFR 20-style regulation) and the arithmetic is blunt: about **six minutes** at one meter from the unshielded source spends a full year's allowance.

That is why *controlling personnel exposure* is not a side duty - it is the central skill of the trade. The controlling relationship is simple:

- **Dose = dose rate × time**

You cannot undo a dose you have already received, so every control acts in advance: lower the rate you stand in (distance, shielding) or shorten the time you stand in it. Typical regulations add further ceilings - about 15 rem/yr to the lens of the eye, 50 rem/yr to skin and extremities, and far lower limits for members of the public - but a radiographer who manages rate and time rarely approaches any of them.

sections.heading: Time: The Bluntest Lever
sections.body:
Dose scales directly with time: half the minutes in the field, half the millirem. Time control sounds trivial, but in the field it is **planning**, not hurry.

- **Dry-run the shot** while the source is locked in the device: walk the guide-tube route, place markers and film, rehearse the crank count - all at zero dose rate.
- **Stage everything** (film, IQIs, tape, the next setup) before the first exposure so you never improvise inside a radiation area.
- **Move with purpose** near the device, and do your waiting at the boundary or beyond it.
- **Respect the small repeated doses.** A film-change routine that keeps you in a 6 mR/h field for 10 minutes costs 1 mR; over thousands of exposures a year that is a steady, avoidable accumulation.

Rushing is not time control - a fumbled connector or a re-shot weld doubles the time spent near the source. Smooth, rehearsed work is the fastest there is. Your direct-reading dosimeter, read and recorded daily, is the scoreboard that tells you whether your time habits are working.

sections.heading: Distance: The Inverse Square Law
sections.body:
Radiation from a compact source spreads in all directions, so the same energy passes through an ever-larger sphere as it travels. Intensity therefore falls with the **square** of distance:

- **I₁d₁² = I₂d₂²**

Double the distance and the rate drops to **one quarter**; triple it and you are at **one ninth**. It runs in reverse too - halve your distance and the rate **quadruples** - which is why the last few steps toward a source are the most expensive ones.

Worked example: the meter reads **8 mR/h at 20 ft** from the exposure point, and the procedure posts boundaries at 2 mR/h. You need a factor of 4, so you need **twice the distance**: post at about **40 ft, then confirm with the survey meter**. The law assumes a point source and no scatter; real shots add scatter from floors, walls, and the part itself, so the calculation predicts and the **meter decides**.

Distance is the cheapest dose reduction on any job: crank from the **full length of the drive cable**, never coil it short for convenience, and choose crank and boundary positions during the dry run - not while the source is out.

sections.caption: The same energy crosses a larger area as distance grows - doubling the distance cuts the dose rate to one quarter.

sections.heading: Shielding: HVL and TVL
sections.body:
Matter attenuates gamma rays exponentially - **I = I₀e^(−µx)** - so practical shielding is handled in **half-value layers (HVL)**, the thickness that cuts the dose rate in half, and **tenth-value layers (TVL ≈ 3.32 HVL)**, which cut it to one tenth.

For **Ir-192**, one HVL is roughly **0.5 in. of steel or about 4.8 mm of lead**; for the harder gammas of **Co-60** it is roughly **7/8 in. of steel or about 12.5 mm of lead** (approximate values - source spectra and geometry shift them).

Stacked layers multiply: 3 HVLs give 1/8 (160 mR/h becomes 20 mR/h); 2 TVLs give 1/100. Need 200 mR/h brought under 2 mR/h? That is a factor of 100 - about **2 TVLs ≈ 32 mm of lead** for Ir-192.

Use the shielding you did not bring: concrete walls, steel vessels, the corner of the building - put structure between you and the exposure point whenever the cable run allows. And treat the **collimator** as shielding you carry to the shot: in its shielded directions it commonly cuts rates by a **factor of 10 or more**, shrinking both the boundary and your own dose at the crank position.

sections.caption: Each half-value layer of lead halves the Ir-192 dose rate; about 3.3 HVLs make one tenth-value layer.
Approximate HVL and TVL values for common radiography sources - treat as planning values and verify against source-specific data.

sections.title: The boundary is a measured line, not a guessed one
sections.body: Post restricted-area boundaries where the **survey meter** reads at or below about **2 mR/h** (typical practice), not where the inverse-square estimate lands. Re-survey whenever the source, collimation, or geometry changes; keep the meter **on and in hand** during every crank-out and crank-in; and **survey after every exposure, on every approach** - crank feel and the position indicator are hints, never proof.

sections.heading: ALARA: A Working Philosophy
sections.body:
**ALARA - As Low As Reasonably Achievable** - is the principle that dose limits are **ceilings, not allowances**. Staying merely legal is not the standard; the standard is every millirem you can reasonably avoid, avoided. Regulators expect licensees to run a documented ALARA program - management commitment, dose-trend reviews, investigation levels that trigger questions well below the limits - but on the job ALARA is a set of habits that stack the three levers:

- **Collimate every shot** the technique allows; never open-beam out of convenience.
- **Crank from full cable length** with structure between you and the exposure point.
- **Schedule** high-dose work for empty shops and off-hours.
- **Brief the crew** so nobody learns the plan inside the boundary.
- **Read your dosimeter daily** and treat any unusual number as a question that needs an answer.
- **Choose the gentler tool** when it still makes the technique - a lower-activity source, a lower-energy isotope, or an X-ray set that shuts off at the flip of a switch.

A radiographer's dose history should look boring - low, flat, explainable. When a week spikes, ALARA means finding the setup or habit that caused it and fixing it, not shrugging because the total is still under the limit.

interactive.title: Dose Reduction Scenario Trainer
interactive.intro: Night shift at a pipe-fabrication shop: six butt-weld exposures with a 60 Ci Ir-192 source in a gamma projector. You are the radiographer; a trainee runs film. Make the calls a Level II would make.

steps.prompt: During setup you measure 8 mR/h at the planned rope line, 20 feet from the exposure point. The procedure posts boundaries at 2 mR/h. What do you do?
options: Post the boundary where it is - 8 mR/h is close enough for short exposures
Move the boundary out to about 40 feet and confirm 2 mR/h or less with the survey meter
Keep the boundary at 20 feet and tell everyone to stand behind it for twice as long
steps.feedback: Inverse square: cutting 8 mR/h to 2 mR/h needs a factor of 4, which means doubling the distance to about 40 ft. Then the meter - not the math - confirms the line, because scatter off floors, walls, and the part distorts the ideal calculation.

steps.prompt: Each shot only needs a narrow beam toward the weld. The collimator is heavy, and it is 2 a.m. Do you rig it?
options: Skip it - collimators only matter in daylight when other crews are around
Skip it, but double the boundary distance to compensate
Rig it - in its shielded directions it cuts dose rates by a factor of 10 or more, shrinking the boundary and your own dose at the crank
steps.feedback: A collimator is shielding you carry to the shot. It reduces the restricted area, the chance of an overexposure to others, and your own accumulated dose - exactly the cheap, repeatable reduction ALARA demands on every exposure, not just the convenient ones.

steps.prompt: Where do you stand while cranking the source out?
options: At the full length of the drive cable, with the building's concrete wall between you and the exposure point
Beside the guide tube, so you can watch the source seat in the collimator
Halfway along the cable run, so the crank-out goes faster
steps.feedback: Stack the levers: maximum distance plus structural shielding, chosen during the dry run. Cranking briskly and smoothly minimizes time while the source is in transit - the highest-rate moments of the whole operation - and your survey meter stays in view throughout.

steps.prompt: The exposure ends and you crank the source back. The drive felt normal and the position indicator shows shielded. Before anyone approaches the guide tube, what does professional practice require?
options: Nothing more - the indicator shows the source is home
Approach with the survey meter on and in view, watching it fall, then survey the device, guide tube, and collimator before touching anything
Wait five minutes for residual radiation to die down, then approach normally
steps.feedback: Indicators and crank feel can lie; only the meter proves the source is shielded. And there is no residual radiation to wait out - when the source is shielded the field is gone, and a high reading on approach means the source is NOT home. Back out and execute your emergency procedure.

steps.prompt: At shift end your pocket dosimeter reads 40 mR - about double your usual night, though far under any limit. What is the ALARA response?
options: Nothing - limits exist so you can use them
Stop doing boundary walk-downs, since walking the boundary added some of that dose
Record it, work out which setup drove the extra dose, and change the layout or habit that caused it
steps.feedback: ALARA treats limits as ceilings, not allowances. An unusual reading is a question that needs an answer - maybe a crank position lost its shielding wall, or a collimator was skipped. Find it, fix it, and the trend line stays boring, which is the goal.

checkQuestions.question: A survey meter reads 80 mR/h at 10 feet from an exposed source. Ignoring scatter, about what will it read at 40 feet?
options: 40 mR/h
20 mR/h
10 mR/h
5 mR/h
checkQuestions.explanation: Distance increased by a factor of 4, so the rate falls by 4² = 16: 80 ÷ 16 = 5 mR/h. The posted boundary is still confirmed by survey, not by arithmetic alone.

checkQuestions.question: A film-change routine keeps you in a 6 mR/h field for 10 minutes on each shot. What dose do you receive per shot?
options: 0.1 mR
1 mR
6 mR
10 mR
checkQuestions.explanation: Dose = rate × time = 6 mR/h × (10/60) h = 1 mR. Small repeated doses like this are exactly what time-control habits are built to eliminate.

checkQuestions.question: About how much lead is needed to bring an Ir-192 dose rate from 160 mR/h down to 20 mR/h?
options: About 14 mm
About 5 mm
About 32 mm
About 48 mm
checkQuestions.explanation: 160 to 20 is a factor of 8 = 2³, so 3 HVLs are needed; at about 4.8 mm of lead per HVL for Ir-192, 3 × 4.8 ≈ 14 mm.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement best captures the ALARA concept?
options: Doses are acceptable as long as they stay below 5 rem per year
Keep every dose as far below the limits as reasonably achievable - limits are ceilings, not targets
ALARA applies only to members of the public, not to radiographers
ALARA requires zero dose on every job regardless of cost
checkQuestions.explanation: ALARA treats regulatory limits as ceilings and demands that reasonably avoidable dose be avoided. The word reasonably acknowledges cost and practicality - but merely staying legal is not the standard.

checkQuestions.question: Typical regulations set the occupational whole-body (TEDE) limit at:
options: 500 mrem per year
2 rem per quarter
5 rem per year
50 rem per year
checkQuestions.explanation: Typical NRC/Agreement-State-style regulations cap occupational whole-body TEDE at 5 rem (50 mSv) per year; the 50 rem/yr figure applies to skin and extremities, not the whole body.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic Radiographic Physics 7.1–7.3
Typical regulations: 10 CFR 20 (dose limits, ALARA program) and 10 CFR 34 (industrial radiography operations)
General radiation protection references: inverse square law, HVL/TVL shielding data for Ir-192 and Co-60
